title: Permission & Obligation in Context
description: Practice using Czech modal verbs in real-life situations — asking for permission, expressing obligation, refusing, and distinguishing between 'must not' and 'don't have to'.

exercises.prompt: Which modal verb do you use to say 'I must' (internal or external obligation)?

data.explanation: 'Muset' = must — expresses obligation or necessity. 'Musím jít' = I must go. 'Moct' = can (ability), 'smět' = may (permission), 'chtít' = to want.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'You must not smoke here' in Czech?

data.question_en: How do you say 'You must not smoke here' in Czech?
data.explanation: 'Nesmíš' = you must not (prohibition). 'Nemusíš' = you don't have to (no obligation). 'Nemůžeš' = you cannot (lack of ability). 'Tady nesmíš kouřit.' = You must not smoke here. The distinction between 'nesmět' (prohibited) and 'nemuset' (not required) is crucial.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the correct modal to ask for permission: 'May I sit here?'

data.explanation: 'Smím si sednout tady?' = May I sit here? 'Smět' is used to ask for permission. 'Můžu/Mohu' (can) is also acceptable in informal contexts, but 'smět' is more precise for permission.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the correct negative modal: 'Children must not run in the corridor.'

data.explanation: 'Děti nesmějí běhat v chodbě.' = Children must not run in the corridor. 'Nesmějí' = they must not (3rd person plural of 'nesmět'). This is a prohibition — 'nesmět' is used, not 'nemuset'.

exercises.prompt: Translate to Czech: 'You don't have to come if you're tired.'

data.source: You don't have to come if you're tired.
data.grammar_note: 'Nemusíš' = you don't have to (no obligation, but allowed). This is different from 'nesmíš' (you must not). 'Přijít' = to come. The distinction between 'nemuset' and 'nesmět' is one of the trickiest points for learners.

accepted_answers: Are you able to help me?
data.grammar_note: 'Můžete' = you can (2nd person plural/formal of 'moct'). 'Pomoci' = to help (infinitive). This is a polite request using the formal 'vy' form.

exercises.prompt: Complete the dialogue at a restaurant.

lines.speaker: customer
lines.text_en: Hello, may we sit on the terrace?
lines.text: Dobrý den, ___ si sednout na terase?

lines.speaker: waiter
lines.text_en: Yes, of course. You can choose a table.
lines.text: Ano, samozřejmě. Můžete si vybrat stůl.

lines.speaker: customer
lines.text_en: Thank you. And may we smoke on the terrace?

lines.speaker: waiter
lines.text_en: Unfortunately, you must not smoke here.

data.explanation: In this dialogue, 'můžeme/smíme' ask for permission. The waiter uses 'nesmíte' to express a prohibition — smoking is not allowed. Note the formal 'vy' form (můžete, nesmíte) used in service interactions.
data.scenario: A customer asks the waiter if they can sit outside.

exercises.prompt: Find the mistake in this prohibition sentence.

data.prompt_en: Find the mistake — wrong modal for a prohibition.
data.explanation: 'Nemusíte' means 'you don't have to' (no obligation), which implies parking is optional. 'Nesmíte' means 'you must not' (prohibition). The correct sentence 'Tady nesmíte parkovat.' = You must not park here. This is a prohibition sign, so 'nesmět' is required.

exercises.prompt: Find the mistake in this permission sentence.

data.prompt_en: Find the mistake — wrong modal verb form.
data.explanation: This sentence is actually correct! 'My můžeme jít do kina.' = We can go to the cinema. 'Můžeme' is the correct 1st person plural of 'moct'. The question mark was the only issue — as a statement, it should end with a period.

exercises.prompt: Match each Czech sentence to its English meaning.

data.prompt_en: Match each Czech modal sentence to its English meaning.
data.explanation: Each modal has a distinct meaning: 'muset' = obligation, 'moct' = ability/possibility, 'smět' = permission, 'nemuset' = no obligation. 'Nemusím' is NOT the same as 'nesmím' (I must not).

exercises.answer_key: Musím jít→I must go., Můžu jít→I can go., Smím jít→I may go., Nemusím jít→I don't have to go.

exercises.prompt: Match each negative modal to its meaning.

data.prompt_en: Match each Czech negative modal to its English meaning.
data.explanation: The three negative modals have distinct meanings: 'nesmět' = prohibition, 'nemuset' = no obligation, 'nemoct' = inability. Confusing 'nesmět' and 'nemuset' is the most common error for learners.

exercises.answer_key: nesmět→must not, nemuset→don't have to, nemoct→cannot, nechtít→don't want to

exercises.prompt: Pronounce this sentence asking for permission politely.
